original_hypothesis.title: Public distribution start can be used to intentionally freeze all transfers by forcing a distribution that cannot complete (gas-bound lock)
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Any account can start distribution once the period elapses, which immediately sets `LockedForDistribution=true` and blocks all transfers/mints/burns. If the holders set is large enough, the distribution cannot be completed quickly, turning the protocol into a transfer-frozen state for an extended time and potentially indefinitely if completion becomes economically infeasible.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Locking is a global circuit breaker triggered by a public function. Combined with holder-array bloat (by normal usage or adversarial duplication), an unprivileged actor can deterministically force a freeze without any transaction-ordering advantage.

proof_of_signal.pattern: distribute(uint256) is public; when starting, _beginDistribution() sets LockedForDistribution=true before iterating; completion requires iterating through `holders`

preconditions: holders array is large enough (or contains many phantom/duplicate entries) such that completing distribution requires many calls
Attackers can pay gas for at least one distribute call to trigger lock

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Global lock is engaged at distribution start, but distribution completion is multi-transaction and depends on bounded iteration.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Whenever MinDistributionPeriod has elapsed; attacker can choose worst operational moments (e.g., during maintenance) without mempool.

operational_questions: What is the maximum holders size for which distribution can be completed within acceptable time/cost?
What monitoring/automation exists to rapidly complete distributions once started?
Is there a fallback/unlock mechanism if distribution cannot complete?
validation_requirements: Simulate worst-case `holders` growth and measure number of distribute() calls required
Check whether incomplete distribution can be rescued if some token transfers revert/return false mid-loop (depends on reward token behavior)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait until MinDistributionPeriod has elapsed (observable on-chain).
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call `distribute(1)` to start distribution and lock transfers.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Do nothing further (or intermittently call distribute with minimal batch sizes) so others must bear cost to unlock by completing distribution.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Repeat each period to maximize downtime.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Global transfer freeze during distribution; mint/burn blocked; users cannot exit positions via transfers
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium to high (requires executing many distribute calls; if economically prohibitive, may require redeploy/migration)
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All ERC20 transfers and supply operations

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Triggered purely by on-chain state; no ordering assumptions

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: DistributionStarted emitted by unknown account; long gaps before DistributionFinished
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Spike in failed user transfers with 'distribution in progress'
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Attacker repeatedly starting distributions with tiny batch sizes

validation_result.mechanism: Publicly triggerable global lock with partial completion
validation_result.impact_summary: Any user can intentionally freeze all token transfers, mints, and burns by starting a distribution with a small batch size, forcing others to pay gas to unlock the contract.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The `LiquidInfrastructureERC20` contract uses a global lock (`LockedForDistribution`) to prevent state changes during revenue distribution. The `distribute(uint256 numDistributions)` function is public and allows any caller to initiate a distribution once `MinDistributionPeriod` has elapsed. When called, `_beginDistribution()` sets `LockedForDistribution = true`, which causes `_beforeTokenTransfer` to revert on any transfer, mint, or burn. 

Because the caller can specify `numDistributions` (e.g., as low as 1), an attacker can start the distribution, process a single holder, and leave the contract in a locked state. The contract remains locked until `nextDistributionRecipient == holders.length`, meaning other users or the protocol administrators are forced to submit and pay for subsequent `distribute()` transactions to finish iterating through the `holders` array and unlock the contract. If the `holders` array is large (which can be exacerbated by the `address(0)` burn bug), this creates a persistent Denial of Service condition that can be re-triggered every time the `MinDistributionPeriod` elapses.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code clearly shows that `distribute` is public, takes an arbitrary `numDistributions` parameter, engages a global lock via `_beginDistribution()`, and only releases the lock when the entire `holders` array has been processed. The `_beforeTokenTransfer` hook strictly enforces this lock for all token movements.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The function allows any user to pass a small `numDistributions` (e.g., 1). If the lock is not active, it calls `_beginDistribution()`. It will only call `_endDistribution()` (which releases the lock) if the loop reaches the end of the `holders` array. A small `numDistributions` leaves the contract locked.
Engages the global lock that halts all token operations.
Enforces the lock on all transfers, mints, and burns, effectively freezing the token while `LockedForDistribution` is true.

execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: When `_isPastMinDistributionPeriod()` is true, an attacker calls `distribute(1)`. The function enters the `if (!LockedForDistribution)` block and calls `_beginDistribution()`, setting `LockedForDistribution = true`. The loop runs for exactly 1 iteration. `nextDistributionRecipient` becomes 1. Assuming `holders.length > 1`, the condition `nextDistributionRecipient == holders.length` is false, so `_endDistribution()` is skipped. The transaction completes successfully, but the contract remains globally locked.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: An attacker monitors the blockchain for `(block.number - LastDistribution) >= MinDistributionPeriod`. As soon as this condition is met, the attacker submits a transaction calling `distribute(1)`. The transaction succeeds, locking the contract. Legitimate users attempting to transfer, mint, or burn tokens will have their transactions reverted with 'distribution in progress'. To restore functionality, legitimate users or the protocol owner must send multiple `distribute()` transactions to process the remaining holders, bearing the gas cost. The attacker can repeat this griefing attack every distribution cycle.
validation_result.impact_assessment: High operational disruption. The protocol's core ERC20 token can be frozen at will by any external actor, requiring manual intervention and gas expenditure from legitimate users to unfreeze. This degrades the reliability of the token and can be used to grief users during critical market moments.
recommended_mitigations: Remove the global `LockedForDistribution` lock and instead take a snapshot of balances/supply at the start of the distribution, allowing transfers to continue while distribution happens asynchronously.
If a lock is strictly required, restrict the initiation of distributions to an authorized role (e.g., `onlyOwner` or a dedicated keeper bot) rather than leaving it public.
Enforce a minimum batch size for `distribute()` (e.g., require processing at least N holders or the remaining array, whichever is smaller) to increase the cost of griefing.
validation_result.replication_plan: Deploy LiquidInfrastructureERC20 with a few approved holders. Mint tokens to multiple holders. Fast forward block number past MinDistributionPeriod. Call distribute(1) from an attacker account. Attempt to transfer tokens from a legitimate holder; observe the revert. Call distribute() repeatedly until the array is finished to observe the unlock.
